note: All scenarios are fictional illustrations for planning purposes only. None represent actual tax advice or guaranteed outcomes.

scenarios.name: BrightPath — C-Corp from Day One
scenarios.description: BrightPath forms directly as a C corporation on day one. Founder issues stock at formation for $10,000 cash. Law version: post-July 4, 2025.

result.notes: Threshold test: $10K << $75M — passes. Original issue: direct formation — likely satisfies. Active business: dependent on revenue mix at time of sale. Holding period: 5 years — satisfies post-2025 rules. Excluded business: uncertain — depends on revenue composition. Professional review required.

scenarios.name: S-Corp Converts at $1M Valuation
scenarios.description: Operating S corporation with $1M estimated value converts to C corporation by revoking S election. Founder issues new shares post-conversion.

result.notes: Original-issue status after S-corp revocation requires professional review. Built-in gains may apply. The holding period runs from conversion date. Threshold at conversion: $400K << $75M — passes. Key uncertainty: does revocation and new issuance satisfy original-issue requirement? Professional tax opinion recommended before relying on QSBS.

scenarios.name: Converts at $5M — Threshold Comfortable
scenarios.description: LLC converts to C corporation via statutory conversion at $5M estimated value. Net assets are $2M (mostly IP and cash). Stock issued at conversion.

result.notes: Aggregate gross assets at issuance: $2M << $75M — passes comfortably. Original issue after statutory conversion: uncertain — requires legal review. Ten-times-basis ceiling: 10 × $500K = $5M. Fixed-dollar limit: $15M. Applicable limit: $15M. If 100% excluded at 5 years: $15M excluded gain. Excluded tax savings vs. 23.8% baseline: approximately $3.57M. Professional review of conversion mechanics required.

scenarios.name: Converts at $15M — Near Threshold
scenarios.description: High-value LLC converts to C corporation. Net assets (cash + software basis) are $12M at conversion. Approaching the $75M threshold.

result.notes: Aggregate gross assets at issuance: $12M < $75M — passes, but leaves only $63M headroom. If company raises additional capital, threshold may be breached for future issuances. Ten-times-basis: 10 × $2M = $20M. Fixed-dollar: $15M. Applicable limit: $20M. If eligible gain is $48M and limit is $20M, approximately $28M taxable gain. Monitoring threshold headroom is critical.

scenarios.name: Converts Near Threshold — $70M Net Assets
scenarios.description: Company converts at high valuation. Net assets are $70M, just under the $75M post-2025 threshold. Risky timing.

result.notes: Aggregate gross assets at issuance: $70M < $75M — barely passes. Any subsequent cash raise before the threshold test date could push over $75M. High risk of disqualification if business raises capital. The $5M headroom is thin. Professional review urgently recommended. Also: at $200M exit on $5M basis, eligible gain is $195M × 80% = $156M. Fixed limit: $15M. Ten-times: $50M. Applicable: $50M. Only $50M excluded; $106M taxable gain remains.

scenarios.name: Above Threshold — Does Not Qualify
scenarios.description: Company's net assets at issuance are $90M, above the $75M threshold. Stock does not qualify for QSBS.

result.notes: Aggregate gross assets at issuance: $90M > $75M threshold. Stock fails the qualified small business test. No QSBS exclusion available. Full gain of $290M taxed at applicable capital gains rates. Federal tax (23.8%): approximately $69.02M. Illustrates the critical importance of timing issuances before the threshold is crossed.

scenarios.name: Post-2025 Stock — 3-Year Hold
scenarios.description: Stock acquired January 2026 under post-July 4 rules. Sold after exactly 3 years.

result.notes: 3-year hold under post-2025 rules: 50% exclusion. Gain: $4.9M. Eligible gain: $4.9M. Fixed limit: $15M (not reached). Ten-times: $1M. Applicable limit: $1M. Excluded: $1M × 50% = $500K. Taxable: $4.4M. Note: the ten-times-basis limitation reduces the benefit significantly at this price. Waiting until year 4 or 5 would improve results if eligible gain is within limits.

scenarios.name: Post-2025 Stock — 4-Year Hold
scenarios.description: Same scenario as sample-007 but held one additional year for 75% exclusion.

result.notes: 4-year hold: 75% exclusion. Gain: $6.9M. Ten-times limit: $1M. Fixed limit: $15M. Applicable: $1M. Excluded: $1M × 75% = $750K. Taxable: $6.15M. Limited benefit due to low stock basis. Basis planning at issuance matters — a higher supportable basis at issuance would increase the ten-times ceiling.

scenarios.name: Post-2025 Stock — 5-Year Hold
scenarios.description: Same founder, stock held 5+ years for 100% exclusion under post-2025 rules.

result.notes: 5+ year hold: 100% exclusion. Gain: $9.9M. Ten-times limit: $1M. Fixed limit: $15M. Applicable: $1M. ALL eligible gain excluded (up to $1M). $8.9M taxable. Federal+NIIT tax on taxable portion: ~$2.12M. Savings vs. no-QSBS baseline on excluded $1M: ~$238K. Note: if exits closer to $1M gain, full tax-free. The ten-times-basis limit drives planning — founders should understand their basis ceiling before assuming a large exclusion.

scenarios.name: Stock Sale vs. Asset Sale Comparison
scenarios.description: Illustrates the double-tax cost of an asset sale vs. a stock sale where the shareholder holds QSBS.

result.notes: STOCK SALE: Gain $9.8M. Ten-times limit: $2M. Fixed limit: $15M. Applicable: $2M. 100% excluded ($2M). Taxable: $7.8M. Tax: ~$1.856M. After-tax: ~$8.14M. ASSET SALE (same deal): Corporation sells assets at $10M, pays 21% corporate tax on gain (~$10M - asset basis). Assume net assets basis $3M: corporate gain $7M, corporate tax $1.47M. After-tax corporate proceeds $8.53M distributed to shareholder. Shareholder pays 23.8% on qualified dividend/liquidation gain (~$8.33M × 23.8% ≈ $1.98M). Shareholder after-tax: ~$6.35M. STOCK SALE ADVANTAGE: ~$1.79M even with partial QSBS limitation.

scenarios.name: BrightPath — Software-Dominant Scenario
scenarios.description: BrightPath scenario where 90%+ of revenue derives from software subscriptions, automated diagnostic delivery, curriculum access, and platform licensing. Minimal human tutoring.

business_qual.notes: Revenue mix strongly favors software and automated delivery. Principal asset appears to be proprietary IP, software, and data infrastructure rather than founder skill or tutor time. Low excluded-business risk in this scenario. Key remaining question: whether any consulting, implementation, or founder-dependent revenue crosses the line into a prohibited service. Professional review recommended but positive facts are strong.

scenarios.name: BrightPath — Human-Tutoring-Dominant Scenario
scenarios.description: BrightPath scenario where 60% of revenue derives from live human tutoring sessions billed per session or per tutor-hour. Software is secondary.

business_qual.notes: Revenue mix heavily weighted toward human tutoring and consulting — categories that may constitute excluded services. Whether this rises to the level of an excluded 'consulting' or 'reputation/skill' business depends on structural facts. If tutors are employees delivering a standardized proprietary curriculum, excluded-business risk may be lower. If tutors are the product, risk is higher. This scenario flags significant uncertainty. Only a qualified tax attorney can advise on the specific facts. Do not assume QSBS applies without professional review.
